entries.full_content:
# 🌐 MiOS — Universal AI Integration
> **Proprietor:** Kabu.ki
> **Infrastructure:** Self-Building Infrastructure (Personal Property)
> **License:** Licensed as personal property to Kabu.ki
> **Source Reference:** MiOS-Core-v2.1.0
---

# MiOS v2.1.0 Detailed Technical Logs
Chronological compilation of push logs and milestone updates.

---
## Source: CHANGELOG-v1_3_0.md
# MiOS v2.1.0 CHANGELOG

**Release Date:** April 6, 2026
**Codename:** Intelligence Update
**Sources:** Universal Blue, Bazzite, Bluefin, SecureBlue, bootc v1.15, Fedora Rawhide fc45

---

## Breaking Changes

- **systemd 260**: cgroup v1 support REMOVED. All services must use cgroup v2.
- **systemd 260**: SysV service scripts no longer supported.
- **GNOME 49+**: systemd is a HARD dependency. gnome-session built-in service manager removed.
- **Kernel 7.0**: kernel-modules-core split from kernel-modules (added to PACKAGES.md).
- **NVIDIA 590**: Pascal (GTX 10xx) support dropped. Open kernel modules default for Turing+.

## Containerfile Improvements

- **dnf5 cache mount** (`--mount=type=cache,dst=/var/cache/libdnf5`): 5-10x faster rebuilds
- **tmpfs mount** (`--mount=type=tmpfs,dst=/tmp`): Prevents /tmp artifacts from bloating layers
- **`/opt → /var/opt` symlink**: Makes /opt writable on immutable filesystem (Universal Blue pattern)
- **`CMD ["/sbin/init"]`**: bootc convention for bootable images
- **Post-build validation**: Verifies critical packages installed, checks for footgun packages
- **Third-party repo disable**: RPMFusion/Terra disabled after build (Bazzite pattern)

## New System Files

- **`/usr/lib/bootc/kargs.d/00-mios.toml`**: Declarative kernel boot arguments via bootc v1.11+ drop-in directory. Ships IOMMU, NVIDIA DRM, and security hardening params (slab_nomerge, init_on_alloc, pti, vsyscall=none) without bootloader modification.
- **`/usr/lib/ostree/prepare-root.conf`**: Enables composefs for verified boot filesystem with content-addressed dedup.
- **`/usr/lib/sysctl.d/99-mios-hardening.conf`**: SecureBlue-style kernel security hardening (kptr_restrict, dmesg_restrict, ptrace_scope, network hardening, fs protection).

## Build System

- **Repo priority hierarchy** (01-repos.sh): CrowdSec(80) < Terra(85) < RPMFusion(90) < Fedora(99) — Bazzite pattern
- **Post-build package validation** (build.sh): Verifies 14 critical packages, flags footgun packages
- **CrowdSec repo** moved from 12-virt.sh to 01-repos.sh for proper initialization order

## PACKAGES.md Fixes

- **Fixed**: Duplicate `pcp` and `pcp-system-tools` entries in Cockpit section
- **Fixed**: Removed `lib32-gamemode` and `libstrangle` (Arch-only, not in Fedora repos)
- **Added**: `kernel-modules-core` (kernel 7.0 split)
- **Added**: `container-selinux` and `k3s-selinux` to new K3s prerequisites section
- **Added**: `nvidia-persistenced` to NVIDIA section
- **Added**: `composefs` and `container-selinux` to containers section

## GPU / VFIO

- **RTX 50-series VFIO reset bug**: Detection in GPU auto-detect service, user warning at boot, documentation at `/usr/share/doc/mios-vfio-warning.txt`
- **NVIDIA open kernel modules**: `nvidia-open.conf` modprobe config for Turing+ default
- **New tool**: `mios-vfio-check` validates IOMMU, vfio modules, NVIDIA GPU detection, RTX 50 warning

## Looking Glass B7

- **`-DENABLE_LIBDECOR=ON`**: Required for GNOME Wayland window decorations
- **`-DENABLE_PIPEWIRE=ON`**: PipeWire audio support
- **Force OpenGL renderer**: Config at `/etc/skel/.config/looking-glass/client.ini` fixes NVIDIA+Wayland flicker
- **KVMFR module**: SELinux policy (`mios_kvmfr`) allows svirt_t access to /dev/kvmfr0

## Security Hardening

- **Kernel boot params**: slab_nomerge, init_on_alloc=1, page_alloc.shuffle=1, randomize_kstack_offset=on, pti=on, vsyscall=none via bootc kargs.d
- **Network sysctl**: tcp_syncookies, accept_redirects=0, send_redirects=0, rp_filter=1, log_martians=1
- **Process isolation**: ptrace_scope=2, unprivileged_bpf_disabled=1, bpf_jit_harden=2
- **File protection**: protected_hardlinks=1, protected_symlinks=1, protected_fifos=2, protected_regular=2

## SELinux

- **New policy**: `mios_portabled` — systemd-portabled D-Bus for sysext/confext (systemd 258+)
- **New policy**: `mios_kvmfr` — Looking Glass shared memory device access for VMs

## K3s

- **Pinned version**: v2.1.0+k3s1 for reproducible builds
- **SELinux**: container-selinux + k3s-selinux installed BEFORE K3s binary
- **Config**: `/etc/rancher/k3s/config.yaml` with selinux: true, traefik disabled

## Service Management

- **Fixed**: pmcd/pmlogger services no longer enabled (only pmproxy is installed)
- **Added**: podman-auto-update.timer for quadlet auto-updates
- **Added**: bootloader-update.service for bootc systems
- **Podman quadlet example**: CrowdSec dashboard at `/usr/share/containers/systemd/`

## Tools

- **Updated**: `mios-update` with localhost origin detection and fix
- **New**: `mios-vfio-check` — VFIO passthrough readiness checker
- **Updated**: `mios()` help function lists all v1.3 tools
- **Updated**: MOTD/banner to v1.3

---

## Files Changed (13 files)

| File | Status | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| VERSION | Modified | v2.1.0 → v2.1.0 |
| Containerfile | Modified | dnf5 cache, tmpfs, /opt symlink, validation, repo disable |
| PACKAGES.md | Modified | Duplicate fix, name corrections, new packages |
| scripts/build.sh | Modified | Post-build validation, summary |
| scripts/01-repos.sh | Modified | Priority hierarchy, CrowdSec repo |
| scripts/02-kernel.sh | Modified | kernel-modules-core, version logging |
| scripts/10-gnome.sh | Modified | GNOME 49+ systemd notes, Bibata v2.1.0, Flatseal |
| scripts/11-hardware.sh | Modified | NVIDIA open modules, RTX 50 warning |
| scripts/12-virt.sh | Modified | LG B7 libdecor, K3s pin, quadlet |
| scripts/20-services.sh | Modified | pmcd fix, podman-auto-update, systemd 260 |
| scripts/99-overrides.sh | Modified | RTX 50 detect, vfio-check tool, SELinux policies |
| push-to-github.ps1 | Modified | v1.3 new file verification |
| system_files/ (3 new) | Added | kargs.d, composefs, sysctl hardening |

---
### ⚖️ Legal & Source Reference
- **Copyright:** (c) 2026 Kabu.ki
- **Status:** Personal Property / Private Infrastructure
- **Project Repository:** [Kabuki94/MiOS](https://github.com/Kabuki94/MiOS)
- **Documentation:** [MiOS Knowledge Base](https://github.com/Kabuki94/MiOS/tree/main/docs/knowledge)
- **Artifact Hub:** [ai-context.json](../../ai-context.json)
---

entries.full_content:
# 🌐 MiOS — Universal AI Integration
> **Proprietor:** Kabu.ki
> **Infrastructure:** Self-Building Infrastructure (Personal Property)
> **License:** Licensed as personal property to Kabu.ki
> **Source Reference:** MiOS-Core-v2.1.0
---

# Changelog
All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-04-25

### 🛠️ Hardware & WSL2 Optimization
* **Intel Battlemage Support:** Implemented strict `xe` driver bindings for Xe2 hardware.
* **WSL2 Stability:** Resolved `dbus-broker` crashes on WSL2 kernels.
* **RTX 50 VFIO fix:** Initial Blackwell GSP firmware workaround deployed.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-04-22

### ⚙️ Core OS & Role Engine Consolidation
* **Unified Role Engine:** Consolidated the asynchronous initialization and role management into the extensionless `system_files/usr/libexec/mios/role-apply` script.
* **Formal Role Targets:** Introduced `mios-{desktop,headless,k3s-master,ha-node}.target` for strict role isolation.
* **Architectural Overlay Purity:** Consolidated all dynamically created systemd units into the `system_files/` overlay. Removed redundant root-level config directories.
* **Next-Gen MOTD:** Enhanced `/usr/libexec/mios/motd` with live Role, MOK (Secure Boot), and Update status indicators.
* **2026 Flatpak Standard:** Adopted `/usr/share/flatpak/pre-installed.d/` for mandatory application delivery.
* **Logging Purity:** Audited and fixed malformed UTF-8/encoding issues in all shell scripts.

### 🏗️ Build & CI/CD Optimization
* **Rechunking Fix:** Optimized the CI pipeline to generate 5-10x smaller updates by running rechunking inside a privileged container.
* **Docs Restructure Fix:** Resolved build failures caused by the relocation of `PACKAGES.md` to `docs/PACKAGES.md`.
* **Build Diagnostics:** Enhanced `packages.sh` with FATAL error logging for mandatory sections.
* **Fix (Virtualization):** Removed `dkms` from `packages-looking-glass-build` manifest to resolve `kernel-devel-matched` dependency conflict on mixed kernel builds.
* **Fix (Scripts):** Hardened `12-virt.sh` to ensure `/usr/share/mios/virtio` exists before VirtIO-Win ISO download.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-04-18

### 🛠️ Hardware & GPU Passthrough
* **NVIDIA 595+ Stability:** Injected `NVreg_UseKernelSuspendNotifiers=1` to fix Wayland freezes on Ada/Blackwell hardware.
* **WSL v2.1.0 Fix:** Gated `systemd-networkd-wait-online.service` on `!wsl` to prevent session timeouts.
* **WSL v2.1.0 Fix:** Enforced 0755 on `wsl-user-generator` to fix login failures.
* **Blackwell Safety Fix:** Implemented `vfio_pci.disable_idle_d3=1` workaround in `kargs.d`.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-04-16

### ⚙️ Driver & Hardware Optimization
* **NVIDIA Open Modules:** Standardized on NVIDIA open kernel modules as the default for Turing+.
* **CDI Generation:** Integrated build-time CDI specification generation for NVIDIA GPUs.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-04-14

### 🖥️ Desktop & UI Evolution
* **GNOME 50 Transition:** Migrated to GNOME 50 (Wayland-only). Removed all legacy X11 session components.
* **DNF5 Build Shift:** Fully transitioned the build pipeline to `dnf5`.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-03-25

### 🚀 Unified Image Architecture
* **Role-at-Boot:** Introduced the `mios-role.service` and `role.conf` system.
* **ublue-os Adoption:** Integrated pre-signed NVIDIA kmods and adopted the `uupd` unified updater.
* **Image Signing:** Implemented `cosign` keyless signing via GitHub Actions.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-03-12

### 🔒 Ecosystem Intelligence & Hardening
* **SecureBlue Integration:** Deployed SecureBlue-inspired kernel and sysctl hardening.
* **Bootloader Management:** Added `bootupd` for unified bootloader updates.

## [v2.1.0] - 2026-02-15

### ⚙️ Core OS Foundation
* **systemd 260 Compliance:** Removed cgroup v1 support and SysV compatibility.
* **composefs Integration:** Promoted root filesystem to `enabled = yes`.

---
### ⚖️ Legal & Source Reference
- **Copyright:** (c) 2026 Kabu.ki
- **Status:** Personal Property / Private Infrastructure
- **Project Repository:** [Kabuki94/MiOS](https://github.com/Kabuki94/MiOS)
- **Documentation:** [MiOS Knowledge Base](https://github.com/Kabuki94/MiOS/tree/main/docs/knowledge)
- **Artifact Hub:** [ai-context.json](../../ai-context.json)
---

entries.full_content:
# 🌐 MiOS — Universal AI Integration
> **Proprietor:** Kabu.ki
> **Infrastructure:** Self-Building Infrastructure (Personal Property)
> **License:** Licensed as personal property to Kabu.ki
> **Source Reference:** MiOS-Core-v2.1.0
---

# MiOS Legacy v1.1 - System Profiler Toolkit
Merged on 2026-04-25

## Update Changelog
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SYSTEM PROFILER TOOLKIT - UPDATE v1.1                 ║
║              Critical Fixes Applied                           ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

Date: January 15, 2026
Version: v2.1.0

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CRITICAL FIX: Proper User Home Directory Detection
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

PROBLEM IDENTIFIED:
──────────────────
When running scripts with sudo, files were being saved to /root/ 
instead of the actual user's home directory, making them 
inaccessible to regular users.

Example of the issue:
  User runs: sudo ./system-profiler.sh
  Files saved to: /root/system-profile/  ❌ Can't access!
  Should save to: /home/username/system-profile/  ✓


THE FIX:
────────
All scripts now properly detect the REAL user even when run with 
sudo and save files to the correct home directory.

Detection Logic:
  1. Check if SUDO_USER is set (running with sudo)
  2. If yes, use SUDO_USER and their home directory
  3. If no, use current USER and HOME
  4. Set proper file ownership to the real user

Code Implementation:
  if [ -n "${SUDO_USER:-}" ]; then
      REAL_USER="$SUDO_USER"
      REAL_HOME=$(getent passwd "$SUDO_USER" | cut -d: -f6)
  else
      REAL_USER="${USER:-$(whoami)}"
      REAL_HOME="${HOME:-$(eval echo ~$REAL_USER)}"
  fi


SCRIPTS UPDATED:
────────────────
✓ system-profiler.sh
✓ run-all-profilers.sh  
✓ cloud-ws-assess.sh
✓ profiler-menu.sh

All scripts now:
  • Detect real user correctly
  • Save to correct home directory
  • Set proper file ownership (chown)
  • Work seamlessly with sudo


OUTPUT LOCATIONS (Fixed):
─────────────────────────
Before (WRONG):
  sudo ./system-profiler.sh     → /root/system-profile/  ❌
  sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh     → /root/cloud-ws-assessment/  ❌
  sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh   → /root/profiler-run/  ❌

After (CORRECT):
  sudo ./system-profiler.sh     → /home/user/system-profile/  ✓
  sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh     → /home/user/cloud-ws-assessment/  ✓
  sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh   → /home/user/profiler-run/  ✓
  sudo ./profiler-menu.sh       → /home/user/profiler-output/  ✓


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
ADDITIONAL FIXES
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. AUDIO CONNECTION ERRORS (Suppressed)
   ────────────────────────────────────
   Issue: PulseAudio "Connection refused" errors
   Fix: Errors are now properly caught and don't clutter output
   
   These errors are harmless when:
   • System has no audio running (headless server)
   • PulseAudio/PipeWire not started
   • Running in TTY without desktop

2. POWER MANAGEMENT WARNINGS (Handled)
   ───────────────────────────────────
   Issue: power-profiles-daemon errors on some systems
   Fix: Gracefully handled with proper error catching
   
   Not critical - only affects battery status on laptops

3. PCIe VPD WARNINGS (Non-Critical)
   ────────────────────────────────
   Issue: "pcilib: sysfs_read_vpd: read failed"
   Status: This is just a kernel warning, not an error
   Impact: None - PCIe information is still collected correctly


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
WHAT YOU NEED TO DO
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✓ NOTHING! All scripts are already updated!

Just use them normally:
  sudo ./system-profiler.sh
  sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh  
  sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh
  sudo ./profiler-menu.sh

Files will now automatically save to YOUR home directory, not root's.


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
VERIFICATION
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Test that it's working:

1. Run a profiler with sudo:
   sudo ./quick-summary.sh

2. Check file location and ownership:
   ls -la ~/profiler-output/
   
3. You should see files owned by YOUR username, not root!

Expected output:
  -rw-r--r-- 1 youruser yourgroup  5.2K Jan 15 14:22 quick-summary-*.txt
                ^^^^^^^^ ^^^^^^^^^ 
                  Your username!


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
VERSION HISTORY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

v2.1.0 (2026-01-15)
─────────────────
  ✓ Fixed: Files save to user's home directory, not /root/
  ✓ Fixed: Proper ownership when running with sudo
  ✓ Improved: Error handling for audio/power services
  ✓ Improved: Better non-critical error suppression

v2.1.0 (2026-01-15)
─────────────────
  • Initial release
  • Comprehensive system profiling
  • 5 core tools + documentation


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
TESTING NOTES
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

The fix has been tested with:
  ✓ Running with sudo
  ✓ Running without sudo
  ✓ Different user accounts
  ✓ MiOS (Arch-based)
  ✓ systemd-boot configuration

All file locations and ownership now work correctly!


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
TECHNICAL DETAILS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

How the fix works:

1. REAL USER DETECTION
   ──────────────────
   Uses $SUDO_USER environment variable (set by sudo)
   Falls back to $USER or whoami() if not using sudo

2. HOME DIRECTORY RESOLUTION  
   ─────────────────────────
   Uses getent passwd to get user's actual home directory
   More reliable than $HOME when using sudo

3. FILE OWNERSHIP
   ──────────────
   Uses chown to set files to real user:group
   Applied after directory creation and file generation
   Recursive (-R) to catch all subdirectories

4. BACKWARDS COMPATIBLE
   ────────────────────
   Works with sudo: ✓
   Works without sudo: ✓
   No breaking changes to existing behavior


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
QUESTIONS?
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Common Questions:

Q: Do I need to re-run profilers?
A: No, the fix only affects WHERE files are saved, not what's 
   collected. Old profiles in /root/ can be manually copied if 
   needed.

Q: Will this work on all distributions?
A: Yes! Uses standard Linux tools (getent, chown, etc.) that 
   are available on all distributions.

Q: What if I don't use sudo?
A: Scripts work fine without sudo too, they'll just have limited
   access to some hardware info that requires root.

Q: Can I still access /root/ files?
A: If you have old profiles in /root/, you can copy them:
   sudo cp -r /root/system-profile /home/youruser/
   sudo chown -R youruser:yourgroup /home/youruser/system-profile


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

                     ALL FIXED! 🎉

            Ready for Cloud-WS Development!

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

## Quick Start Guide
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║        SYSTEM PROFILER TOOLKIT - QUICK START GUIDE           ║
║              For Cloud-WS Development                         ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

📦 INCLUDED TOOLS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. system-profiler.sh       - Comprehensive system analysis
2. iommu-visualizer.sh      - IOMMU group visualization
3. quick-summary.sh         - Fast system overview
4. profile-compare.sh       - Compare two profiles
5. README.md               - Full documentation


🚀 QUICK START
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STEP 1: Quick Check (30 seconds)
─────────────────────────────────
./quick-summary.sh

This gives you:
  • System readiness for Cloud-WS
  • Critical component check
  • IOMMU/GPU status
  • Quick pass/fail verdict


STEP 2: IOMMU Analysis (if using GPU passthrough)
──────────────────────────────────────────────────
sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh

This shows:
  • All IOMMU groups
  • GPU isolation status
  • PCIe device topology
  • USB controller groups
  • Passthrough recommendations


STEP 3: Full Profile (recommended before setup)
────────────────────────────────────────────────
sudo ./system-profiler.sh

This collects everything:
  • Complete hardware inventory
  • All drivers and modules
  • Package list
  • BIOS/UEFI parameters
  • Thermal and power info
  • 22+ detailed sections

Output: ~/system-profile/system-profile-TIMESTAMP.txt


📋 TYPICAL WORKFLOWS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WORKFLOW 1: New System Assessment
──────────────────────────────────
./quick-summary.sh                 # Initial check
sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh         # GPU passthrough check
sudo ./system-profiler.sh          # Full baseline


WORKFLOW 2: Pre-Installation
─────────────────────────────
sudo ./system-profiler.sh          # Baseline profile
grep -i "iommu\|virt\|kvm" ~/system-profile/system-profile-*.txt
# Review output, plan configuration


WORKFLOW 3: Troubleshooting
────────────────────────────
sudo ./system-profiler.sh          # Current state
./profile-compare.sh old-profile.txt new-profile.txt
# Find what changed


WORKFLOW 4: Hardware Database
──────────────────────────────
# Run on each test system:
sudo ./system-profiler.sh

# Build compatibility matrix:
mkdir hardware-db
mv ~/system-profile/*.txt hardware-db/
# Document: system-name-YYYY-MM-DD.txt


🎯 WHEN TO USE EACH TOOL
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

quick-summary.sh
  ✓ Quick health check
  ✓ Before purchasing hardware
  ✓ After BIOS changes
  ✓ Daily monitoring

iommu-visualizer.sh
  ✓ Planning GPU passthrough
  ✓ Checking PCIe isolation
  ✓ USB controller analysis
  ✓ Passthrough troubleshooting

system-profiler.sh
  ✓ Pre-installation baseline
  ✓ Post-installation verification
  ✓ Before major updates
  ✓ Documentation/support tickets
  ✓ Hardware compatibility DB

profile-compare.sh
  ✓ Before/after kernel updates
  ✓ Driver changes
  ✓ Troubleshooting regressions
  ✓ Comparing test systems


💡 PRO TIPS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. Always use sudo for complete data
   sudo ./system-profiler.sh

2. Save profiles with meaningful names
   cp ~/system-profile/system-profile-*.txt baseline-profile.txt

3. Extract specific sections
   grep -A20 "IOMMU GROUPS" profile.txt

4. Search across all profiles
   grep -i "nvidia" ~/system-profile/*.txt

5. Compare before major changes
   sudo ./system-profiler.sh  # Save before changes
   # Make changes
   sudo ./system-profiler.sh  # Save after
   ./profile-compare.sh before.txt after.txt


🔍 COMMON SEARCHES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Find GPU info:
  grep -i "vga\|3d\|nvidia\|amd" profile.txt

Check IOMMU status:
  grep -i "iommu" profile.txt

View loaded modules:
  grep -A50 "LOADED KERNEL MODULES" profile.txt

Check virtualization:
  grep -i "kvm\|qemu\|virt" profile.txt

Find TPM status:
  grep -i "tpm" profile.txt

Check Secure Boot:
  grep -i "secure boot" profile.txt


🛠️ INSTALLATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Scripts are ready to use! Optional: install to PATH

sudo cp system-profiler.sh /usr/local/bin/system-profiler
sudo cp iommu-visualizer.sh /usr/local/bin/iommu-visualizer
sudo cp quick-summary.sh /usr/local/bin/quick-summary
sudo cp profile-compare.sh /usr/local/bin/profile-compare

Then run from anywhere:
  system-profiler
  iommu-visualizer
  quick-summary


📦 RECOMMENDED PACKAGES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

For complete functionality:

Arch/MiOS/EndeavourOS:
  sudo pacman -S lshw hwinfo inxi smartmontools lm_sensors \
                 ethtool dmidecode usbutils pciutils neofetch

Debian/Ubuntu:
  sudo apt install lshw hwinfo inxi smartmontools lm_sensors \
                   ethtool dmidecode usbutils pciutils neofetch


⚙️ CLOUD-WS SPECIFIC CHECKS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Before Cloud-WS setup, verify:

1. IOMMU enabled
   grep "IOMMU: ENABLED" profile.txt

2. GPU isolated
   sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh
   # Look for "Isolated GPUs (good for passthrough)"

3. KVM available
   grep "KVM: Available" profile.txt

4. TPM present (for Win11 VMs)
   grep "TPM Device: Present" profile.txt

5. Sufficient resources
   grep "Mem:" profile.txt
   # Check for 16GB+ RAM


📊 OUTPUT LOCATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

system-profiler.sh     → ~/system-profile/system-profile-TIMESTAMP.txt
iommu-visualizer.sh    → Screen output + export option
quick-summary.sh       → Screen output only
profile-compare.sh     → Screen output only


🎓 LEARNING RESOURCES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Understanding IOMMU Groups:
  https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/PCI_passthrough_via_OVMF

GPU Passthrough Guide:
  https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/PCI_passthrough

KVM/QEMU Documentation:
  https://www.linux-kvm.org/page/Main_Page


⚠️ IMPORTANT NOTES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

• Some commands require sudo/root for hardware access
• Profiles may contain serial numbers - review before sharing
• Large profiles (>10MB) can be compressed with gzip
• Run sensors-detect before first use for thermal data
• IOMMU must be enabled in BIOS + kernel parameters


📞 SUPPORT & FEEDBACK
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

For Cloud-WS development questions:
  • Include system profile output
  • Specify hardware (CPU, GPU, motherboard)
  • Note IOMMU group configuration
  • List any error messages

## Chaining Guide
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║        HOW TO CHAIN & RUN PROFILER SCRIPTS                    ║
║           Multiple Ways to Use the Toolkit                    ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝


You now have FOUR different ways to run the profiler tools!


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
METHOD 1: INTERACTIVE MENU (EASIEST & MOST FLEXIBLE)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✨ RECOMMENDED FOR: First-time users, exploratory analysis

Command:
  $ sudo ./profiler-menu.sh

Features:
  • Interactive menu interface
  • Choose which tools to run
  • See what's already been run (checkmarks)
  • View results without leaving menu
  • Compare profiles from menu
  • Quick system status check
  • Built-in help

Perfect when:
  - You want control over what runs
  - Learning the tools
  - Only need specific checks
  - Want to compare multiple profiles


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
METHOD 2: SIMPLE CHAIN RUNNER (FULLY AUTOMATED)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✨ RECOMMENDED FOR: Complete automated profiling

Command:
  $ sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh

What it does:
  1. Runs quick-summary.sh
  2. Runs iommu-visualizer.sh
  3. Runs system-profiler.sh
  4. Generates comprehensive summary report

Output:
  ~/profiler-run-TIMESTAMP/
    ├── 00-SUMMARY.txt
    ├── 01-quick-summary.txt
    ├── 02-iommu-analysis.txt
    └── 03-full-system-profile.txt

Time: 5-8 minutes total

Perfect when:
  - Need complete documentation
  - Documenting new hardware
  - Creating baseline before changes
  - Want everything in one go


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
METHOD 3: CLOUD-WS ASSESSMENT (SMART ANALYSIS)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✨ RECOMMENDED FOR: Hardware validation & compatibility

Command:
  $ sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh

What it does:
  1. Runs all profilers
  2. Analyzes results intelligently
  3. Scores system 0-10
  4. Provides verdict (EXCELLENT/GOOD/PARTIAL/NOT READY)
  5. Lists specific recommendations

Output:
  ~/cloud-ws-assessment-TIMESTAMP/
    ├── 00-COMPATIBILITY-REPORT.txt  ⭐ START HERE!
    ├── 01-quick-summary.txt
    ├── 02-iommu-analysis.txt
    └── 03-full-profile.txt

Time: 5-8 minutes total

Perfect when:
  - Validating new hardware for Cloud-WS
  - Need pass/fail verdict
  - Want actionable recommendations
  - Assessing compatibility


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
METHOD 4: MANUAL (MAXIMUM CONTROL)
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✨ RECOMMENDED FOR: Specific checks, troubleshooting

Commands:
  $ ./quick-summary.sh              # 30 seconds
  $ sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh      # 1-2 minutes  
  $ sudo ./system-profiler.sh       # 3-5 minutes
  $ ./profile-compare.sh old.txt new.txt

Perfect when:
  - Only need one specific check
  - Troubleshooting specific issue
  - Comparing before/after
  - Building custom workflow


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
WHICH METHOD SHOULD YOU USE?
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ YOUR SITUATION                → RECOMMENDED METHOD            │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ First time using tools        → profiler-menu.sh             │
│ New hardware validation       → cloud-ws-assess.sh           │
│ Complete documentation        → run-all-profilers.sh         │
│ Just checking GPU passthrough → iommu-visualizer.sh          │
│ Quick health check            → quick-summary.sh             │
│ Daily monitoring              → quick-summary.sh             │
│ Before/after comparison       → Manual (run twice)           │
│ Troubleshooting issue         → profiler-menu.sh             │
│ Building hardware database    → run-all-profilers.sh         │
│ CI/CD integration             → Manual scripts               │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
EXAMPLE WORKFLOWS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

SCENARIO 1: New System - First Check
─────────────────────────────────────
$ sudo ./profiler-menu.sh
# Choose option 1 (Quick Summary)
# Review results
# If good, choose option 4 (Run All)


SCENARIO 2: Hardware Validation
────────────────────────────────
$ sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh
# Wait 5-8 minutes
# Read 00-COMPATIBILITY-REPORT.txt
# Get clear verdict and score


SCENARIO 3: GPU Passthrough Planning
─────────────────────────────────────
$ sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh
# Interactive menu appears
# Choose option 2 (GPU analysis only)
# Check for "Isolated GPUs"


SCENARIO 4: Complete Documentation
───────────────────────────────────
$ sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh
# Generates 4 comprehensive reports
# Perfect for baseline documentation


SCENARIO 5: Troubleshooting After Update
─────────────────────────────────────────
# Before update:
$ sudo ./system-profiler.sh
$ cp ~/system-profile/system-profile-*.txt before-update.txt

# After update:
$ sudo ./system-profiler.sh

# Compare:
$ ./profile-compare.sh before-update.txt \
    ~/system-profile/system-profile-*.txt


SCENARIO 6: Daily Monitoring
─────────────────────────────
$ ./quick-summary.sh
# Takes 30 seconds
# Shows any major changes


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
CUSTOM CHAINING IN YOUR OWN SCRIPTS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

You can easily integrate these tools into your own scripts:

Example 1: Pre-Installation Check
──────────────────────────────────
#!/bin/bash

echo "Checking system before Cloud-WS installation..."

# Run quick check
if ! ./quick-summary.sh | grep -q "System ready"; then
    echo "System not ready! Check requirements."
    exit 1
fi

# Check GPU passthrough
if sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh --no-menu | grep -q "Isolated GPUs"; then
    echo "GPU passthrough: READY"
    ENABLE_PASSTHROUGH=true
else
    echo "GPU passthrough: LIMITED"
    ENABLE_PASSTHROUGH=false
fi

# Create baseline
sudo ./system-profiler.sh

# Continue with installation...


Example 2: Conditional Profiling
─────────────────────────────────
#!/bin/bash

# Always do quick check
./quick-summary.sh

# Only profile if GPU detected
if lspci | grep -q "VGA"; then
    echo "GPU detected - analyzing IOMMU..."
    sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh --no-menu
fi

# Full profile on Sundays
if [ "$(date +%u)" -eq 7 ]; then
    echo "Sunday - running full profile..."
    sudo ./system-profiler.sh
fi


Example 3: Parallel Execution (Advanced)
─────────────────────────────────────────
#!/bin/bash

# Run multiple systems in parallel
for host in ws1 ws2 ws3 ws4; do
    (
        echo "Profiling $host..."
        ssh $host 'bash -s' < run-all-profilers.sh > "${host}-results.txt"
    ) &
done

wait
echo "All systems profiled!"


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
AUTOMATION & SCHEDULING
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Weekly Full Profile (Crontab):
  0 2 * * 0 /path/to/system-profiler.sh

Daily Quick Check (Crontab):
  0 8 * * * /path/to/quick-summary.sh > /tmp/daily-check.txt

On Boot Check (systemd):
  [Unit]
  Description=System Profile on Boot
  
  [Service]
  Type=oneshot
  ExecStart=/path/to/quick-summary.sh
  
  [Install]
  WantedBy=multi-user.target


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
OUTPUT MANAGEMENT
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

All outputs go to predictable locations:

profiler-menu.sh       → ~/profiler-output/
run-all-profilers.sh   → ~/profiler-run-TIMESTAMP/
cloud-ws-assess.sh     → ~/cloud-ws-assessment-TIMESTAMP/
system-profiler.sh     → ~/system-profile/

Compress old profiles:
  $ gzip ~/system-profile/*.txt

Clean old runs (keep last 10):
  $ cd ~/system-profile
  $ ls -t | tail -n +11 | xargs rm -f


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
QUICK REFERENCE COMMANDS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Most Interactive:
  $ sudo ./profiler-menu.sh

Most Automated:
  $ sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh

Most Complete:
  $ sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh

Fastest:
  $ ./quick-summary.sh

Best for GPU:
  $ sudo ./iommu-visualizer.sh


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
PRO TIPS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

1. Use interactive menu when learning the tools
2. Use automated runners for regular profiling
3. Always use sudo for complete information
4. Save profiles before major changes
5. Use cloud-ws-assess.sh for hardware validation
6. Profile regularly to track changes over time
7. Compare profiles to troubleshoot issues


═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NEED HELP?
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Each script has built-in help:
  ./script.sh --help

Full documentation:
  cat README.md
  cat TOOLKIT-OVERVIEW.txt

Quick start:
  cat QUICKSTART.txt

## Fix Reference Card
╔═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║           QUICK FIX REFERENCE CARD - v1.1                     ║
╚═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝


🔴 BEFORE (v1.0) - THE PROBLEM
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Running with sudo saved files to ROOT's home directory:

  $ sudo ./system-profiler.sh
  
  Saved to: /root/system-profile-20260115_142211/  ❌
            └─ You can't access this as regular user!

  $ ls ~/system-profile/
  ls: cannot access '/home/youruser/system-profile': 
      No such file or directory  ❌


🟢 AFTER (v1.1) - THE FIX
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Now saves to YOUR home directory, even when using sudo:

  $ sudo ./system-profiler.sh
  
  Saved to: /home/youruser/system-profile-20260115_142211/  ✓
            └─ You CAN access this!

  $ ls ~/system-profile/
  system-profile-20260115_142211/  ✓
  
  $ ls -la ~/system-profile/system-profile-*/
  -rw-r--r-- 1 youruser yourgroup  156K Jan 15 14:22 report.txt  ✓
                ^^^^^^^^ ^^^^^^^^
              Owned by YOU!


📍 NEW FILE LOCATIONS
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

ALL scripts now save to your home directory:

system-profiler.sh
  /home/youruser/system-profile/system-profile-TIMESTAMP/
  
cloud-ws-assess.sh
  /home/youruser/cloud-ws-assessment-TIMESTAMP/
  
run-all-profilers.sh
  /home/youruser/profiler-run-TIMESTAMP/
  
profiler-menu.sh
  /home/youruser/profiler-output/


✅ WHAT'S FIXED
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

✓ Files save to correct user home directory
✓ Files owned by correct user (not root)
✓ Works with sudo AND without sudo
✓ No more "permission denied" when accessing results
✓ Audio errors properly suppressed
✓ Power management warnings handled gracefully


🚀 READY TO USE
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Just run normally:

  sudo ./system-profiler.sh       ✓ Works!
  sudo ./cloud-ws-assess.sh       ✓ Works!
  sudo ./run-all-profilers.sh     ✓ Works!
  sudo ./profiler-menu.sh         ✓ Works!

Files will be in YOUR home directory and owned by YOU!


📝 QUICK TEST
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Verify it works:

1. Run: sudo ./quick-summary.sh

2. Check: ls -la ~/profiler-output/

3. You should see files owned by YOUR username!
